event: He is Risen — The First Easter
symbols: radiant angel at the tomb — the divine messenger announcing the Resurrection
sleeping or fallen soldiers — worldly power rendered helpless before God's victory over death
blossoming tree — new life springing from the Resurrection, the reversal of death's barrenness
women approaching — the faithful women who were first witnesses of the empty tomb
golden light — the glory of the Resurrection transforming darkness into dawn
description: A richly atmospheric scene set in a garden at dawn. At the right, a luminous white-robed angel figure glows with brilliant light beside a stone tomb or doorway. At the left, two women in dark robes stand or crouch beneath a blossoming tree, looking toward the angel. In the foreground, Roman soldiers in armor lie sprawled on the ground, unconscious or terrified. The scene is bathed in a golden-warm light that radiates from the angel, contrasting with the deeper shadows of the garden. Blossoms and flowers are scattered throughout.
theological_description: The Resurrection of Christ is the cornerstone of Christian faith — without it, as Paul declares, 'our preaching is in vain and your faith is in vain' (1 Corinthians 15:14). This painting captures the moment the women arrive at the tomb to find it empty and an angel declaring, 'He is not here; He has risen' (Matthew 28:6). The radiant angel embodies the divine glory that breaks into the world at Easter. The fallen soldiers represent every form of earthly power — military, political, institutional — rendered helpless before God's decisive act. The blossoming tree is a profound symbol: where the tree of the cross brought death, the tree of Easter brings life. The women, marginalized in their society, are chosen as the first witnesses — God's preference for the humble and overlooked. The entire scene vibrates with the conviction that death has been swallowed up in victory (1 Corinthians 15:54).
